channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Immigration Slowdown Hits Every Metro Area in the U.S., Census Shows
item.description: Large urban counties and the border were the most affected. And in three-quarters of U.S. counties, population growth either slowed or turned negative.
item.dc:creator: Jeff Adelson and Amy Qin
item.media:description: The population of Los Angeles County, the country’s most populous, declined by nearly 54,000.

item.title: Trump Calls for Law Cracking Down on Crime and ‘Rogue Judges’
item.description: Ahead of the midterms, President Trump told a Republican gathering that he wants to go after repeat offenders and “rogue judges that are criminals.”
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump and top aides have been railing against judges who have ruled against him, including justices on the Supreme Court.

item.title: ICE Agents at Some Airports Begin Checking IDs in Security Lines
item.description: It was unclear whether having agents helping with screening passengers would improve wait times. Some travelers expressed worries.
item.dc:creator: Christina Morales, Christine Chung, Hamed Aleaziz, Sean Keenan and Rowan Moore Gerety
item.media:description: Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents at Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport in Atlanta on Wednesday.

item.title: Justice Dept. Settles Flynn’s Wrongful Prosecution Suit for $1.25 Million
item.description: The agreement was an extraordinary example of how the Trump administration has offered legal relief to those aligned with the president.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer
item.category: Russian Interference in 2016 US Elections and Ties to Trump Associates
item.media:credit: Tom Brenner for The New York Times
item.media:description: Michael T. Flynn, seen in 2018, briefly served as President Trump’s national security adviser in his first term.

item.title: Judge Orders Arrest of Matt Bevin, Former Kentucky Governor, for Contempt
item.description: Seven years after leaving office, Matt Bevin, the combative ex-governor, faces charges for not providing financial records sought by his estranged son.
item.dc:creator: Campbell Robertson
item.media:credit: Luke Sharrett for The New York Times
item.media:description: Matt Bevin at his election night party in Louisville, Ky., after losing his re-election bid in 2019.

item.title: Trump Draws Bipartisan Backlash for Easing Oil Sanctions on Russia and Iran
item.description: Republicans and Democrats alike have criticized the Trump administration’s moves, taken to stabilize oil markets rocked by the war with Iran, warning that it is benefiting two U.S. adversaries.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A tanker carrying Russian oil in Gujarat, India, earlier this month. The Trump administration has defended the lifting of restrictions on Russia and Iran as necessary to prevent further spikes in energy prices that could ripple through the global economy and hit American consumers.

item.title: U.S.P.S. Plans to Impose 8% Surcharge to Offset Rising Transportation Costs Amid Iran War
item.description: The temporary price increase, which would take effect on April 26, comes amid a spike in fuel prices tied to the war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A post office in Philadelphia. The agency delivers to roughly 168 million addresses, covering every state, city and town.

item.title: Can Rep. Thomas Massie, Leading Trump Critic, Hang On in Kentucky Primary?
item.description: Can any Republican disagree with Trump and get away with it? Thomas Massie’s primary race in Kentucky is a key test.
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck
item.media:credit: Jon Cherry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Thomas Massie on his farm in Garrison, Ky.

item.title: Army Raises Enlistment Age Limit to 42 and Eases Marijuana Rules
item.description: The new enlistment requirements were made official years after the Army faced a recruiting crisis, and as the United States is engaged in a war with Iran.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
Center for a New American Security
item.media:description: The new rules bring the Army’s age requirements more in line with other branches of the military, including the Air Force and Navy.

item.title: James Talarico Responds With ‘Love’ To Hegseth Pastor’s Criticism
item.description: James Talarico, the Democratic nominee for Senate in Texas, said, “You may pray for my death, Pastor, but I still love you.” The pastor said he was calling for Mr. Talarico’s religious conversion.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk and Elizabeth Dias
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: James Talarico at his election night party this month in Austin, Texas.

item.title: Melania Trump Appears With a Robot, Saying More Children Should Be Educated by Them
item.description: The first lady, Melania Trump, believes that more children should be educated by “humanoid educators.”
item.dc:creator: Katie Rogers
item.media:description: Melania Trump, the first lady, with Figure 3, a humanoid robot developed by Figure AI, an artificial intelligence robotics company.

item.title: What to Know About Democrat Emily Gregory’s Win in Florida
item.description: A mother of three won a statehouse seat that includes Mar-a-Lago, while a union electrician leads in a State Senate race in conservative West Tampa.
item.dc:creator: David W. Chen
item.media:credit: via Emily Gregory for Florida campaign
item.media:description: Emily Gregory, a first-time candidate, won in Palm Beach County, Fla., not by focusing on her most famous constituent but by castigating leaders in Tallahassee, the state capital.

item.title: U.S. Circulates Iran Peace Plan While Sending Troops to the Middle East
item.description: The 2,000 paratroopers heading to the region may give President Trump more leverage in negotiations, but they also leave him with the option of doubling down on military force.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump in the Oval Office on Tuesday.

item.title: For 2nd Time, an Appeals Court Backs the Trump Administration’s Detention Policy
item.description: Courts are weighing whether the administration can hold undocumented immigrants without bond, an issue that may be resolved by the Supreme Court.
item.dc:creator: Mattathias Schwartz
item.media:description: Federal agents making an arrest in Minneapolis in January. Should the Trump administration’s policy continue to win support at the appellate level, it would have major implications for millions of people in the United States who crossed the border illegally years ago.

item.title: With Their Voter Bill Stymied, G.O.P. Leaders Ponder a Plan B
item.description: Republicans are eying a last-ditch procedural maneuver to overcome united Democratic opposition, but the chances for success are slim.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.media:description: Senator John Thune, the majority leader, brought the SAVE America Act to the floor under intense pressure from President Trump and conservatives who maintained that it was needed to prevent noncitizens from voting,

item.title: Iran’s Attacks Force U.S. Troops to Work Remotely
item.description: Iran has severely damaged several American military bases in the Middle East, officials say.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: A satellite image of Al Udeid Air Base in Qatar in February.

item.title: Homeland Security Talks Hit Snag as Democrats Demand ICE Restrictions
item.description: A day after Republicans offered to remove money for ICE enforcement from a Department of Homeland Security funding bill, Democrats insisted that the deal must include curbs on federal agents.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Chuck Schumer said a Democratic counteroffer would “rein in” Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents “with common-sense guardrails.”

item.title: Some States Already Preparing for Potential Supreme Court Ban on Late Ballots
item.description: The court’s conservatives appear skeptical of laws allowing mail ballots to arrive after Election Day. A decision could come as late as June, potentially scrambling midterm elections.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Chase Stevens/Las Vegas Review-Journal, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Francisco Aguilar, the Nevada secretary of state, is among those preparing for a potential change to voting rules close to elections.

item.title: Trump Had His Eye on China, Then Plunged Into a New Mideast War
item.description: The Trump administration said the Middle East would “recede” in importance as the China challenge took priority. But the president started the war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: A military parade in Beijing last year. Three American presidents have entered office vowing to focus resources on the challenges posed by China. Each instead turned to crises in other parts of the world.

item.title: Democrats Have Fundraising Edge in Virginia Redistricting Battle Ahead of April Referendum
item.description: Republicans are cautiously optimistic about a statewide referendum now at the center of the country’s gerrymandering war, but Democrats have a huge cash advantage.
item.dc:creator: Campbell Robertson and Theodore Schleifer
item.media:description: Virginians for Fair Elections, the main Democratic-aligned effort to redraw congressional maps in Virginia, has heavily out-raised the main Republican-aligned effort, Virginians for Fair Maps.

item.title: A Critical Political Season Could Decide if Alaska Is a Failed ‘Petrostate’
item.description: A governor who spent two terms cutting services to preserve Alaskans’ oil-funded annual checks is leaving office. Voters must now decide what comes next for the state’s faltering fiscal model.
item.dc:creator: Anna Griffin and Ruth Fremson
item.media:description: Juneau, Alaska, on a snowy morning earlier this month.

item.title: Supreme Court Sides With Internet Provider in Copyright Fight Over Pirated Music
item.description: Leading music labels sued Cox Communications for failing to terminate accounts of subscribers flagged for distributing copyrighted music.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:description: At issue for the justices was whether providers like Cox could be held legally responsible if they knew that customers were pirating music but did not take steps to terminate their internet access.

item.title: Why Are Delays at Houston’s Bush Airport Worse Than at Others?
item.description: Lines were long at airports nationwide Tuesday amid T.S.A. staffing shortages, but passengers at Bush Intercontinental Airport waited more than four hours to pass through checkpoints.
item.dc:creator: Christina Morales
item.media:description: The George Bush Intercontinental Airport in Houston last week. Travelers waited more than four hours on Tuesday to go through security checkpoints.

item.title: Why This City’s Reckoning With Cesar Chavez Is So Complicated
item.description: San Jose, Calif., is where Mr. Chavez began organizing in the 1950s, and he once lived there. After revelations of sexual abuse by the labor leader, the city and his old neighborhood are confronting his legacy.
item.dc:creator: Tim Arango
item.media:credit: Mike Kai Chen for The New York Times
item.media:description: At San Jose City College, the area’s community college, a 40-foot mural depicting Cesar Chavez adorns the library named for him.
